# Tidal Locking

Tidal locking is when an orbiting body always shows the same face to the body it
orbits, the way the Moon always shows the same side to Earth. It happens because
gravity raises a bulge on the smaller body; friction from that bulge gradually slows
its rotation until the rotation period matches the orbital period.

## Consequences for worlds

A tidally locked planet has a permanent day side and a permanent night side, which
can create extreme temperature differences and strong winds between them. Many moons
in the Solar System are tidally locked to their planets, and close-in exoplanets
around small stars are expected to be locked too, which affects whether they could
hold liquid water and support life.
